chapters.title: Reasons to be careful while seeking a higher purpose
chapters.text:
The development of technology isn't the only landmark of our digital age. Due to political and social instability, more and more people question traditional institutions, including the government and church. It's especially true for millennials (born from 1981 to 1996). For example, in the US, around 20% reported practicing no religion in 2012; seven years later, this number reached 40%, according to Pew Research.

Yet it doesn't mean people have a lesser yearning for spirituality now; the opposite is true. With the influence of slogans like “follow your path,” numerous people ask themselves, “Who am I, and what is my mission?”. This quest gets even more complicated when they embark on it alone. Unfortunately, it's a reality for the majority as the level of loneliness is unprecedented. Given these circumstances, it's no surprise that many searching for their calling have turned to alternative sources of purpose and belonging.

Indeed, many communities genuinely strive to help people become the best versions of themselves. However, some can manipulate seekers' innermost aspirations to gain power or make a mint.

A message has immense power over us when it appeals to our intimate wishes and needs.

A similar situation has already happened in American history. It's no coincidence that cultlike movements had numerous followers during the highly unsteady 1960s-70s. Some may believe all these people were brainwashed and such communities have nothing to do with us. However, the truth is much more complicated.

Consider the following:

• A personality test that reveals what impedes us from realizing our potential.
• Volunteering in a close-knit town where people support each other.

What do they have in common? Both can be hooks for cultish movements' fishing rods. So, if you want to avoid being caught, join our investigation.

chapters.title: The pillars of cultish movements
chapters.text:
Before we dive deep into cultish movements, let's clarify their definition to be on the same page. Initially, the word cult didn't have any negative connotations. It derives from Latin cult- “inhabited, cultivated, worshiped,” and its linguistic family includes nouns like culture and cultivation. When it came into usage in English, it broadly denoted worship.

In the early nineteenth century, when the US became a safe place for people with different viewpoints, cult started to signify something new and unconventional. It wasn't until 1969 that the word acquired its dark side. That year, society was shaken by the murders committed by members of the Manson Family cult. Yet as time passed, cult received new connotations — something mysterious or even cool, so the phrases “cult film” and “cult classic” were common.

To decipher people's beliefs, focus on the unusual words they use.

As a result, the current meaning of cult is very broad: it can denote some communities of celebrity fans or be synonymous with sect. That's why Amanda Montell perceives cultish movements as a vast spectrum, uniting groups with some set beliefs, rules, and rituals. On one side, organizations force people to obey leaders, even at the cost of their lives. The other end of the range includes teams claiming to teach extraordinary skills. Indeed, these communities differ enormously, and we can accept Montell's definition or not, yet the main point is that these groups share similar features:

• Arguments that someone is special: Claims like “you live on a higher vibration” are powerful tools, appealing to people's needs to be valued. Besides, who doesn't want to feel like a chosen one?
• A template for identity: In the 21st century, people repeatedly hear they can be anyone, but these countless options often bring pressure instead of happiness. Cultish movements simplify the choice, offering members a higher purpose and belonging.
• The crucial role of language: Although words like “fiber-lab” (denoting laundry room among cultish group Heaven's Gate) or “Roosters” (sports classes in the early morning) belong to different communities, their role is the same — to be the foundation of a particular ideology and a password for insiders.

Did you know? In the 1830s, Americans used the word cult to denote, for instance, communities of science lovers or vegan farming. The latter appeared in Massachusetts thanks to Amos Bronson Alcott, whose daughter Louisa May became a famous writer.

chapters.title: Signs of groups we should avoid
chapters.text:
At the first stop of our journey through the spectrum of cultish movements, we meet Laura. Having grown up in a well-adjusted family, she was always the life and soul of the party. Yet the older she became, the more she noticed racial injustice and decided to fight against it. So, she left college at the end of the 1970s, striving to join civil rights activism.

Having heard about the Peoples Temple Agricultural Project (Jonestown), she believed her dreams came true: a settlement in harmony with nature where people of all races lived peacefully. In fact, they could if not for their leader Jim Jones, whom members called “father.” Using manipulative techniques, he made his followers feel special and claimed that only he could ensure people a better life.

Yet when Jonestown flopped, he refused to admit it. Instead, he told members that the enemy was approaching, and they should commit “a revolutionary act,” which actually meant collective suicide. People had little choice; Jones' militia would kill them if they didn't drink the poison. Laura miraculously avoided that fate, working in another city for cult purposes, but over 900 members didn't.

Nothing is wrong with being idealistic unless others exploit this feeling for their immoral aims.

What were some of the tools used to catch people in ideological nets?

• Love-bombing: The leader of Jonestown inspired people, depicting their great future, listened to them, and gave advice so that they felt understood.
• US vs. THEM rhetoric: Groups nurtured members' feelings of superiority over those who weren't followers; others were considered enemies or fools. It's a typical way to suppress or devalue opposite points of view and sometimes even justify aggression toward THEM. Words strengthened this dichotomy: the followers were called "Jones' children,“ while others — ”traitors“ or ”outside forces."
• Dramatization: Exaggerating and generalizing are commonplace in public speeches of cult leaders.
• Thought-terminating phrases: Special clichés, like “it doesn't matter on a cosmic level,” stopped people from asking questions.

A common stereotype is that these tricks work the best on people who go through challenges and feel lost. Yet the stories of Laura and cults' “HRs” confirm that these groups have one more target audience — active, generous, or idealistic people.

chapters.title: Not each “knowledge” is beneficial for us
chapters.text:
The next point of the spectrum is controversial religions, and Cathy knows them from her personal experience. She was an aspiring actress in 1991, so the artists’ group Scientology, recommended by a friend, sounded like a wise choice to hone her skills. The fact that the church organized it seemed an advantage to Cathy; she dreamed of finding a higher purpose.

At first, the group offered her affordable self-improvement materials on topics like the ebbs and flows of life, which worked effectively. Participants also told her that she could leave the community anytime. Nothing hinted at underlying motives.

Gradually, Scientologists introduced specific words, such as PTP (current challenge), bypassed charge (return of previous negative energy), 1.1 (hidden hostility), and other terms from two dictionaries they compiled. Using these words, the group aimed to make Cathy devalue her previous knowledge and doubt her worldview. Unique wording strengthened her belief that Scientologists possessed superior knowledge, igniting her wish to gain these skills and do what others couldn't.

Practice reading between the lines, as cultish movements don't state their intentions directly.

The group also constantly checked whether she told police officers or lawyers about it. The community called them and others who could harm its reputation PTS — potential trouble sources. However, Cathy didn't see these questions as interrogation at the time. She was eager to “go clear” — achieve enlightenment of the highest level — and was ready to pay thousands of dollars for it. Yet, as soon as she reached one level, she discovered the next; they were endless, in contrast to her money.

Why did Cathy quit only 18 years later? The first reason is group pressure. She feared disappointing all her friends and gave up on her aspirations. Secondly, the community told her that stopping on the path to enlightenment could bring her misfortune. Although she overcame these superstitions, some believed they risked their lives if they left. Besides, the group had a thought-terminating cliché to reject all accusations: if something went wrong, people had to blame themselves — they made it happen.

Did you know? To hide the hierarchy, members of another religious community, Shambhala, represent it as a mandala, not a pyramid, where those closer to the center have more privileges.

chapters.title: The group that sells hope of financial independence
chapters.text:
People knew almost nothing about the mentioned cultish movements before joining them. The pyramid scheme, by contrast, is far more famous. Still, it doesn't always protect people from enlisting its sibling, multilevel marketing (MLM), or direct sales, a milder version of a cultish movement.

Its history started after the end of World War II. When men returned from the frontline to their jobs, many working women became housewives and missed the feeling of independence. Some didn't hesitate to exploit this nostalgia. For instance, selling food containers, single mom Brownie Wise soon realized that other women, who were her target audience, could also become distributors. It was a game-changing idea.

Instead of waiting for kind words from others, love-bomb yourself with words of support and appreciation.

Nowadays, the target audience of direct sales has changed: single moms, marginalized people, inexperienced students, and religious groups are a gold mine for pyramid schemes. However, the promises of direct sales, like in the past, rest on people's desire to work on themselves and earn a good living. So, recruiters often use words from the business field, such as affiliates (not employees) or commissions (not wages), and love-bomb people with messages like “You have the power to lead!” “Build your business empire!” and “You are a born boss!”

Moreover, the initial investment is small. People need no work experience and simply buy a kit with samples of overpriced beauty or wellness eco-products. They then attract ten new members per month, which will be their downline. This term means people whose tiny commissions (from kits or products they sell) will be sent to those who recruited them. The trick is that to earn a good living, a person needs a very broad downline, achievable only for those at the top of the pyramid. The rest are in red, with hundreds of unsold products in their drawers.

Why do people join these schemes, even knowing about the pitfalls? Some do it from despair or a need to improve their self-esteem, while others believe they can outsmart the system. At the same time, the roots of this problem are even more profound. In societies that equal people's success and their value, there is constant pressure for achievements. The fear of failing in a career fuels the MLM's model, making its business of selling hope to people sound even more captivating.

chapters.title: How did gyms become places for seeking our true selves?
chapters.text:
Remember going to the gym and talking about muscles, calories, and hydration? Now, doing the same exercises, you may be called a worrier; you aren't simply training — you move to empowerment. Welcome to “cult fitness.” Indeed, it occupies the opposite end of the spectrum than the movements discussed earlier. Yet some of its tricks will sound quite familiar.

So, how did the sport become inspirational? The seeds of this change date to the late '80s and '90s, when yoga came to the US and brought the idea that movement could cause physical and inner transformation. Yet, on a bigger scale, this idea developed at the beginning of the 21st century.

Nightclub lighting, ballet themes, and motivational quotes on the walls are only some of the elements companies use to achieve an anti-gym atmosphere. They position themselves as movements with specific rules, rituals (e.g., group photos at the end of training), and clothes. Indeed, they also use special words: some call their centers "boxes" while others rename exercises, like punches and lunges, into courage and commitment.

Additionally, instructors in these centers are more than teachers; they are mentors. They listen to people, support them at difficult times, and accompany training with inspiring speeches. Many clients go to these gyms solely to listen to them, as they boost their self-worth.

Meaning-making is a growth industry. ~ Casper ter Kuile, a writer

At the same time, even this type of cultish movement has its risks:

• Overreliance on instructors: Sometimes, people don't stop training even when they feel discomfort or pain because their mentors say to continue. For instance, clients could become unconscious in the hot yoga classes of Bikram Choudhury.
• Cults of trainers: The main reason why people prolong their memberships is that some instructors have significant influence and occasionally overstep the boundaries of morality. Trainers can mistreat some of their colleagues and clients they don't like, facing no punishment.
• Pyramid schemes: At times, instructors love-bomb their clients, saying how talented they are and offering them to sign up for the course and become trainers. Yet after people spend more than a thousand dollars on it, they can't find a job.

Don't let a stranger's supportive words cloud your critical thinking. Always ask yourself, “Does this person need something from me?”

chapters.title: A fertile soil for cultish movements
chapters.text:
Years ago, it seemed unthinkable that people could adhere to harmful ideas without leaving their houses. Nowadays, it's ubiquitous. Indeed, not all content on the Internet is hazardous, but some resources aim to alter beliefs in a certain way. This type of cultish movement is just one click away from us — the button named “Follow.”

It wasn't the aim of the web, yet we should admit it does provide indecent people with infrastructure to spread their ideas. They no longer need costly hooks to recruit new members and then count money losses as many people quit immediately after joining. With the large amount of data we all leave while surfing the net, web algorithms already know who is susceptible to ideas of cultish movements. They allow communities to reach potential members and avoid those who may not suit them. It's the power of targeting.

As a result, we have the boom of fake gurus or scientists who offer to deal with problems alternatively, like healing our DNA. In a sense, they, like other cultish movements, sell people hope to change their lives and use one of the most potent tools for this aim — language. For instance, they often use words from fields that aren't common knowledge, like neuroscience terms, and combine them with spiritual promises.

How can someone check their statements without having a degree? The easiest solution is to investigate whether the author has a PhD in it. Yet, we shouldn't click the first link in our search as it usually leads to a personal website. We must dig deeper, like the website of the university the author claims to have a degree from and other more reliable sources.

Before believing some “scientists” or “gurus,” research what their opponents state about their theories.

Another cultish movement popular online relates to conspiracy theories. They tap into people's need for simple answers to sophisticated issues, giving them a feeling of control and that they grasp what happens. In times of uncertainty, it's worth a lot. For instance, instead of researching the reasons for the crisis, it's much easier to say that rich people have intentionally planned it. Indeed, they have a lot of power, yet other factors are also crucial. Simple answers won't guide us to the truth.

In a social media–centered society, we've all been rendered at once cult leader and follower. ~ Amanda Montell

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
According to some philosophers, we all feel lost in the world. A few know who they are, what direction they should choose, why certain events happen, and how to cope with them. Everyone figures out these answers by themselves, choosing their paths, sometimes leading to cultish movements. Not all of them are dangerous, but knowing what we can encounter on our path to healing or fulfilling our dreams is essential.

That's because few are immune to manipulative techniques. Indeed, high education and critical thinking may help, but believing in something is a highly complex process with many factors. For instance, theoretically, a person with a childhood based on trust can be more prone to cult movements than one with a difficult time as a kid. Yet, if the latter goes through hardship or is impulsive, the chances of falling into something unusual rise.

Given these numerous factors, instead of counting how many chances we have to join cultish movements, let's recap some signs of manipulation:

• Excessive appreciation: No matter how much we need supportive words, we should be alert when strangers use them. Statements about our superiority and the need to abandon our friends or relatives are also ominous signs.
• Lack of transparency: If the group fears others' points of view or questions, it has something to hide. The ethical movement is clear about what it wants from its members and allows them to exit anytime.
• Vague words: Linguistic tools can be used to make us confused and dependent on cult leaders.

Try this


• Read more about techniques used to persuade people and learn to recognize them in speech.
• Monitor your mental health.
• Every day, remind yourself what a wonderful person you are. You don't need others' validation.

Read it till the end?
